abstract: This study examines how political parties and leaders in Norway, Sweden, and Denmark used Facebook during recent national elections (Norway 2021, Sweden 2022, Denmark 2022), focusing on the emotional 'Love' and 'Angry' Reactions and their relationship to sharing behavior. Using CrowdTangle data on posts from parliamentary parties and leaders, the authors analyze posting activity (supply), emotional reactions (demand), and shares (mobilization). They find that gratitude and celebratory posts attract 'Love' reactions, while populist parties' confrontational posts attract 'Angry' reactions, and that 'Angry' reactions are positively associated with sharing while 'Love' reactions are negatively associated with sharing.
key_claims: Emotional reactions on Facebook ('Love' and 'Angry') function as affective affordances that shape the visibility and virality of political posts during elections.
Populist parties in Scandinavia are particularly successful at eliciting angry reactions and at mobilizing shares of their content.
Posts evoking anger tend to be shared more, whereas posts evoking love tend to be shared less, suggesting negative emotions drive mobilization on Facebook.

Despite similarities among Scandinavian media and political systems, Sweden stands out for higher posting volume by populists (notably the Sweden Democrats) and a more even balance between love and anger engagement.
Posts from parties outperform posts from individual party leaders in generating shares across all three countries.
methods: Quantitative comparative analysis of Facebook posts from parliamentary parties and party leaders in Norway (2021), Sweden (2022), and Denmark (2022), covering six weeks before and one day after each election.
Data collected via CrowdTangle, archived 26 April 2023, yielding 1,609 Danish, 1,676 Norwegian, and 2,629 Swedish posts with metadata on reactions, shares, comments, and likes.
Actor-centered classification of parties as populist or non-populist using Inglehart & Norris (2016), the 2017 Chapel Hill Expert Survey, and the PopuList (Rooduijn et al., 2019).
Visual analysis via scatterplots (Figures 4–6) mapping posts by Love, Angry, and Shares to identify outlier/highly engaged posts, followed by qualitative inspection of those posts' themes.

Three negative binomial regression analyses (one per country) predicting shares from reactions, populist status, and actor type (party vs. leader), reporting Nagelkerke pseudo R² (Sweden = .21, Norway = .23, Denmark = .24).
findings: Swedish political actors posted roughly twice as much as Norwegian or Danish actors; the Sweden Democrats averaged 7.3 posts/day versus 2 for Norway's Progress Party and 1.8 for Denmark's Inger Støjberg.

Posts expressing gratitude for electoral support and anniversary celebrations attracted the highest 'Love' reactions, typically posted by mainstream/non-populist parties (Social Democrats in Sweden and Denmark, Labour and the Red Party in Norway).

Populist parties (Sweden Democrats, Denmark's Hard Line, Norway's Progress Party) received the most 'Angry' reactions, particularly on posts criticizing immigration and energy policies and confronting opponents.

Denmark's 'Love' scale was largest (a Mette Frederiksen thank-you post received ~14K hearts); Sweden showed a more balanced Love/Angry distribution; Norway produced the single most-shared post (~7,500 shares) from Centre Party leader Vedum on national electricity policy.

Regression results: 'Angry' reactions positively predict shares across all three countries (strongest in Norway); 'Love' reactions negatively predict shares in Norway (~7% less) and Denmark (~12% less), non-significant in Sweden.
Posts from party leaders rather than parties were associated with fewer shares (3% less for Sweden and Norway, 6% less for Denmark); populist status had an unexpected negative coefficient in Norway.
contributions: Provides one of the first explicitly comparative analyses of Facebook Reactions across the three Scandinavian countries during national elections.
Extends research on emotional virality by linking discrete Reaction types ('Love', 'Angry') to sharing behavior, demonstrating asymmetric mobilization effects.
Empirically supports the claim that populist communication style benefits disproportionately from social media's affective affordances even in low-polarization multiparty systems.
Offers a methodological template combining scatterplot-based outlier identification with negative binomial regression for studying viral political content.
Suggests coding categories (gratitude/celebration vs. criticism/confrontation/call-to-action) for future content analyses of emotional political posts.
framing: The paper positions itself within political communication research on the 'emotional turn' (Papacharissi, Wahl-Jorgensen) and on affective publics and platform affordances, engaging with debates about populist communication style (de Vreese et al., Mudde) and network media logic (Klinger & Svensson). It responds to calls for comparative research on digital political communication across political systems, addressing a gap in studies of emotion-driven engagement in less polarized, multiparty Scandinavian democracies.
